name: Fairfax County Public Schools
districtFact: FCPS's Thomas Jefferson High School for Science and Technology (TJ) is consistently ranked the #1 public high school in the United States. TJ admission is competitive and open to students from Fairfax, Loudoun, Arlington, and Prince William counties — but the application window opens in October of 8th grade and closes by winter. Families who move to Northern Virginia in January are too late for the current-year TJ cycle. Separately, FCPS Advanced Academic Programs (AAP) Level IV referrals begin in September — families who believe their child qualifies should initiate a referral within weeks of school starting, not months later.

about.title: About Fairfax County Public Schools
about.content:
Fairfax County Public Schools was established in 1954 and today serves approximately 181,000 students across more than 200 schools — the largest school district in Virginia and one of the ten largest in the United States. The district covers Fairfax County, a suburban jurisdiction immediately west of Washington D.C. that functions as one of the primary residential hubs for the federal government workforce.

FCPS's community profile is shaped by federal employment more than almost any other large school district in the country. The CIA's headquarters are in Langley, within county limits. The National Reconnaissance Office (NRO), Department of Homeland Security, and dozens of major defense contractors are based in or immediately adjacent to the county. Security clearance transfers and federal agency reassignments arrive year-round, and FCPS is experienced processing mid-year enrollment from families on assignment schedules that don't align with the August calendar.

Approximately 100 languages are spoken by FCPS students — a figure that reflects not just immigrant families but the children of diplomats, international organization staff (the IMF and World Bank draw global families to the D.C. suburbs), and multinational corporate employees. The district's Jewish population is large enough that FCPS observes Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur as school holidays district-wide — a practice unusual outside the New York and D.C. metro areas that consistently surprises families relocating from other states.

Fairfax County's median household income and education levels are among the highest of any county in the United States. Housing prices reflect this: the median home in Fairfax County exceeds $700,000, and for many families the specific school assignment — not just the county — is a primary factor in the purchase or rental decision.

FCPS high school zones map directly to Fairfax County's distinct communities. Langley High School serves Great Falls and the north McLean corridor — historically the highest-income attendance zone in FCPS. McLean High School draws from McLean and the Tysons-adjacent neighborhoods along the Silver Line. Oakton High School, routinely FCPS's highest-ranked school outside TJ, serves Vienna and Oak Hill. Chantilly High School covers the Centreville and South Riding corridor along Route 50. South Lakes High School draws from Reston, a planned community with a dense concentration of STEM-employed families near the Reston Town Center tech corridor. Woodson and Lake Braddock serve the Burke and Annandale communities of the eastern county; West Springfield draws from Springfield and the Beltway's southern quadrant. Madison High School serves the Vienna and Tysons-south communities; Robinson Secondary draws from Fairfax and Burke; Marshall High School serves the Falls Church and McLean-west corridor. Most families researching FCPS check not just the high school zone but also the assigned elementary school — the two don't always share the same reputation or performance profile within FCPS.

about.title: Academic Programs and School Choice
about.content:
FCPS operates one of the most extensive academic program networks in the country, and most of the competitive programs run on application timelines that punish late arrivals.

Thomas Jefferson High School for Science and Technology (TJHSST, or simply 'TJ') is FCPS's flagship selective high school and the top-ranked public high school in the United States by most national rankings. TJ admits students from Fairfax, Loudoun, Arlington, and Prince William counties through a competitive application process. The application window opens in October of 8th grade and closes in winter — families who arrive in January are too late for the current cycle and must wait until the following fall. Admission rates are typically below 20%. A student accepted to TJ will leave their regular FCPS attendance-zone school for the TJ campus in Alexandria.

FCPS Advanced Academic Programs (AAP) provide differentiated instruction at all grade levels. Level IV — the most intensive track — requires a formal referral initiated by a teacher or parent, followed by a district review process. The referral window typically opens in September at the start of the school year. Families who believe their child qualifies should initiate a referral as soon as possible in September, not wait until spring. Late referrals are processed but may delay placement by a full year.

FCPS also operates International Baccalaureate (IB) programs at select high schools, Governor's School programs, career and technical education (CTE) academies, and dual enrollment partnerships with Northern Virginia Community College (NOVA). Many of these programs have their own application deadlines separate from standard enrollment. Contact the FCPS office at fcps.edu to confirm current program availability and application timelines before choosing a home address.

about.title: Before You Move to Fairfax County Public Schools
about.content:
Four things families must do before committing to a Fairfax County address.

Verify your school assignment by exact address — and be aware of the City of Fairfax exception. Fairfax County and the City of Fairfax are separate jurisdictions in Virginia. Despite sharing a name, the City of Fairfax has its own independent school system (Fairfax City Public Schools) that is not part of FCPS. Boundaries between the two systems run through residential neighborhoods. Use the FCPS school finder at fcps.edu/find-a-school with your exact address to confirm which system and which campus serves your home.

Map the TJ and AAP application windows before your move date. If your child is in 7th or 8th grade and TJ is a goal, the application opens in October. Moving to Northern Virginia in November gives you a narrow window; arriving in January means missing the cycle entirely. AAP Level IV referrals open in September. For academically advanced students, arriving before September matters — plan accordingly.

Plan for a late June school year. FCPS ends in mid-June — one of the latest end dates in Virginia and significantly later than Southern and Texas districts. Summer camps that start June 1, childcare programs that transition in late May, and travel plans built around a Memorial Day school-year-end will not work for FCPS families. Adjust summer logistics before the move.

Add Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur to your childcare calendar immediately. Both Jewish High Holidays are FCPS school holidays district-wide — no school, buses, or aftercare. The dates shift by 10–15 days each year based on the Hebrew lunar calendar (typically mid-September to early October). Families from states that don't observe these holidays as school days are regularly caught off guard. Pull the current-year FCPS calendar at fcps.edu as soon as it's published each spring and add both dates before fall childcare arrangements are finalized.

content: FCPS observes Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur as student holidays each fall — no school, buses, or aftercare on either day. The exact dates shift every year with the Hebrew calendar (typically mid-September to mid-October). Pull the FCPS calendar each spring and add both dates to your childcare plan before fall arrangements are finalized.

Cherry blossom peak in Washington D.C. often overlaps with FCPS Spring Break — the National Mall, Tidal Basin, and downtown D.C. hotels fill up fast during this window. Check the FCPS calendar as soon as it's published each spring and book D.C. trips well in advance.

D.C. metro spring break dates vary by district and can shift year to year. FCPS and Loudoun sometimes align on spring break, but Maryland districts and Virginia Beach often do not. Never assume — compare each district's official calendar.

FCPS attendance boundaries shift regularly due to continued Northern Virginia growth. If you've recently moved, confirm your school assignment directly at fcps.edu rather than assuming based on neighbors.

Families moving from Southern states or Texas are often surprised that FCPS ends in mid-June — two to three weeks after districts they're used to. Summer camps that start June 1, childcare that transitions in late May, and travel booked for the last week of May will all conflict with active FCPS school days. Adjust summer plans before the move, not after.

If your child is in 7th grade and TJ is on your radar, the 8th-grade application window opens in October. Moving to Northern Virginia in September gives you a full cycle; arriving in December does not. Map your move date against the October TJ deadline before finalizing relocation timing.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fairfax County Public Schools the same as George Mason University (GMU)?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fairfax County Public Schools (FCPS) is the K-12 public school system serving Fairfax County. George Mason University (GMU) is a separate public research university in Fairfax, Virginia, with its own semester academic calendar. GMU's schedule has no connection to FCPS school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why does Fairfax County observe Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur as school holidays?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS observes Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur as school holidays district-wide, reflecting the district's significant Jewish student population in Northern Virginia. Both dates shift annually because they follow the Hebrew lunar calendar — they typically fall somewhere between mid-September and mid-October, but the exact dates change every year. Check the current FCPS academic calendar at fcps.edu each spring to add them to your childcare schedule before fall. This practice is common in the D.C. metro area but unusual nationally — families relocating from other states are regularly caught off guard.

districtFaqs.q: Does FCPS follow the same calendar as Loudoun County Public Schools?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS and Loudoun County Public Schools are separate school divisions with their own academic calendars. The two districts sometimes share the same Spring Break week, but this is not guaranteed every year — individual teacher workdays, early-release days, and non-school days regularly differ even when spring break aligns. Families coordinating schedules between FCPS and Loudoun — or with childcare providers that serve both counties — should pull and compare both full calendars each year rather than assuming they match.

districtFaqs.q: Does FCPS serve the City of Fairfax?
districtFaqs.a: No. The City of Fairfax is an independent jurisdiction in Virginia with its own separate school system — Fairfax City Public Schools — that is not part of Fairfax County Public Schools. Despite sharing a name, the two systems have different school boards, calendars, and enrollment areas. Families living in the city of Fairfax (not the county) should contact Fairfax City Public Schools directly. The boundary between FCPS and Fairfax City schools runs through the middle of several neighborhoods — verify your division using your exact address.

districtFaqs.q: Why does FCPS end school so late in June?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS's mid-June last day is one of the latest in Virginia and later than most comparable large districts nationally. The late end date reflects the district's calendar structure, which includes additional holidays (Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur in fall) and teacher workdays distributed throughout the year. The exact last day shifts slightly each year — check the current FCPS calendar at fcps.edu. Families planning summer camps or travel should note that FCPS typically ends two to three weeks later than many Southern and Texas districts — summer programs that start June 1 or earlier will conflict with active FCPS school days.

districtFaqs.q: Do FCPS attendance boundaries change often?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Fairfax County has experienced significant population growth and new school construction over the past decade, requiring the school board to redraw attendance boundaries on a regular basis. Boundary adjustments are typically announced in late fall for implementation the following school year. Families who have recently moved — or who are planning to move — should verify their assigned school at fcps.edu using their specific address each year before the school year begins. Do not assume your school assignment is the same as a neighbor's, even on the same street.

districtFaqs.q: How does FCPS handle snow days and inclement weather closures?
districtFaqs.a: Northern Virginia experiences ice storms more often than heavy snowfall, and Fairfax County's road network — with its hilly terrain and mix of shaded roads — can create hazardous bus routes even when accumulation is modest. FCPS builds a set number of inclement weather days into its calendar each year. When closures exceed those days, FCPS may convert scheduled teacher workdays to instructional days or extend the school year. Closure decisions are announced by approximately 5 AM on the affected morning through FCPS's notification system, local radio, and the fcps.edu website. Families should register for FCPS alerts before November — the first ice event of the season often arrives before Thanksgiving.

districtFaqs.q: When does kindergarten registration open for FCPS?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS kindergarten registration typically opens in February for the following fall school year. Virginia requires children to be five years old by September 30 to enter kindergarten — one month later than Texas's September 1 cutoff, which occasionally affects families relocating between states with children born in October or November. Required documents: certified birth certificate, current Virginia immunization record, and proof of Fairfax County residency. Families relocating to Fairfax County in spring or summer should contact their assigned elementary school directly rather than waiting for a district-wide registration event.

districtFaqs.q: How do I enroll a child mid-year in FCPS?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS accepts mid-year enrollment year-round at the school assigned to your address. Required documents: proof of age (birth certificate), current Virginia immunization records, proof of FCPS residency (lease, utility bill, or mortgage statement), and prior school academic records. FCPS will typically accept unofficial transcripts or parent-provided summaries on a provisional basis while official records are requested — useful for families on federal government PCS orders or defense contractor relocations where official records may arrive weeks after the family. Families with children on IEPs or 504 plans should bring full documentation; FCPS has experience with rapid placement for federal security clearance families.

districtFaqs.q: What is FCPS's AAP Level IV program and how do I get my child referred?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS Advanced Academic Programs (AAP) Level IV is the most intensive academic track, providing full-time differentiated instruction for qualifying students beginning in 3rd grade. Placement requires a formal referral initiated by a teacher or parent, followed by a district-level review of academic records, assessments, and teacher input. The referral window typically opens in September at the start of each school year — families who believe their child qualifies should submit a referral request to the school's AAP resource teacher within the first month of school, not later in the year. Families relocating mid-year can submit referrals on enrollment, but placement decisions may be deferred to the following year depending on the timing. Contact FCPS's Department of Advanced Academics at fcps.edu for current referral procedures.

FCPS school start and end times vary by school level — they are not uniform across the district. Generally, high schools start earliest (around 7:20–7:50 AM and dismiss around 2:15–2:50 PM), middle schools start mid-range (around 7:30–8:20 AM), and elementary schools start latest (around 8:15–9:10 AM, dismissing around 3:00–4:00 PM). The staggered bell schedule coordinates with FCPS transportation routes, which serve all three school levels in sequence. Specific start and end times for a given school are listed on that school's page at fcps.edu. Families selecting between neighborhoods should check the actual start times for their assigned school — a 7:20 AM high school start affects morning childcare and commute logistics significantly.

FCPS provides bus transportation for most students, though eligibility depends on distance from school. Students who live beyond the walk-zone threshold (generally 1.5 miles for elementary and 2 miles for secondary schools) are eligible for bus service. Students within those distances are expected to walk or receive car drop-off. FCPS buses operate on staggered schedules tied to the district's three bell schedule tiers — high school routes run first, then middle school, then elementary. New families should check bus eligibility and stop locations through their assigned school after enrollment is complete. Families relocating to FCPS in summer should verify bus assignments before the August school start — route assignments are typically available in late July.

districtFaqs.q: Are school meals provided in FCPS?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. FCPS provides breakfast and lunch at all schools through the district's cafeteria program. Meal eligibility — free, reduced-price, or full-price — is based on household income and is determined through the annual FCPS meal benefits application, available at fcps.edu each summer before the school year begins. Families should apply before school starts to ensure eligibility is in place for the first day. FCPS cafeteria menus are published monthly on the fcps.edu website. Students can also bring a lunch from home. Families new to FCPS should set up a meal account through MySchoolBucks, the district's online payment system, before school starts in August.

districtFaqs.q: What are FCPS early release days and when do they occur?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS schedules early release days throughout the school year — typically 1–2 hours of early dismissal — to allow teachers to participate in professional development and collaborative planning. These days appear on the official FCPS calendar at fcps.edu and are not the same as a school holiday; students attend school for part of the day. The number and dates of early release days vary by year. Childcare providers and afterschool programs may or may not extend coverage to early release dismissal times — verify your provider's policy before the school year starts. Early release days are among the most overlooked calendar details for families new to FCPS.

districtFaqs.q: When is FCPS high school graduation?
districtFaqs.a: FCPS high school graduation ceremonies typically take place in late May and early June, before the official last day of school. Each of FCPS's 25 high schools sets its own graduation date and venue — TJ, Langley, McLean, Oakton, Chantilly, Woodson, Robinson, Madison, Lake Braddock, South Lakes, West Springfield, Marshall, and others each hold separate ceremonies. Graduation dates are typically announced in the spring semester. Because FCPS ends its school year in mid-June (later than most Virginia peers), the graduation season extends into June — families planning travel around graduation should verify their specific school's date before booking. Check your school's website or contact the main office for current-year graduation details.

districtFaqs.q: How many absences are allowed in FCPS before action is taken?
districtFaqs.a: Virginia law requires students to attend at least 90% of instructional time, and FCPS tracks both excused and unexcused absences. When a student accumulates 5 or more unexcused absences, FCPS sends a notice home and may convene a meeting. Absences beyond 10 total (excused or unexcused) in a school year can trigger an attendance review and may require medical documentation for continued excused status. Parents should report any absence in advance or within 24 hours with a written note or phone call to the school's attendance line. For extended absences due to illness, students may be eligible for homebound instruction. FCPS attendance policies are published at fcps.edu.

livingHere.intro: Fairfax County sits at the center of the Washington D.C. federal employment zone, drawing a uniquely international and highly educated population of government workers, defense contractors, and technology professionals. Families relocating here typically come from other states or countries and find a dense suburban county with strong schools, high housing costs, and a commute culture defined by the Beltway.

highlights.label: Federal Agency Headquarters and Mid-Year Enrollment
highlights.detail: The CIA (Langley), NRO, DHS, and dozens of major defense contractors are based in Fairfax County. Security clearance reassignments and federal agency transfers can happen at any time of year — FCPS regularly processes mid-year enrollment from federal employees and contractors arriving outside the August window. Families relocating on federal assignment should contact FCPS enrollment before their start date. Families with children on IEPs or 504 plans should bring documentation; FCPS is experienced with rapid placement for security clearance families.

highlights.label: Washington Dulles Airport (IAD) and School Breaks
highlights.detail: Dulles International Airport is 15–30 minutes from most FCPS neighborhoods and is the primary departure point for FCPS family travel. The school calendar drives the three peak booking windows: Thanksgiving week, the December winter break, and Spring Break (often aligns with Cherry Blossom peak in D.C. — check the current FCPS calendar for exact dates). IAD's international routes — to South Asia, East Asia, Europe, and Latin America — are popular among FCPS's globally connected community and sell out months in advance for school holiday periods. Book spring break international flights immediately after the FCPS calendar is released each spring.

highlights.label: I-495 Beltway Commuting
highlights.detail: The Capital Beltway and I-66 define daily life — rush-hour backups regularly extend 60–90 minutes. Many FCPS families rely on Metro's Silver Line (Tysons to Dulles corridor) to avoid highway congestion.

highlights.label: Tysons Corner & Amazon HQ2
highlights.detail: Tysons is Fairfax's downtown-equivalent employment center; Amazon HQ2 in nearby National Landing has added thousands of tech workers to the region's commuter population since 2023.

highlights.label: Diverse Immigrant Communities
highlights.detail: Fairfax hosts large Korean, South Asian, Latin American, and Ethiopian communities — reflected in the school system's 100+ spoken languages and the district's observance of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur as school holidays.

highlights.label: Housing, Boundaries, and School Assignment
highlights.detail: Fairfax County median home prices exceed $700,000, and for many families the school assignment is a primary factor in the housing decision. FCPS attendance boundaries are redrawn regularly as Northern Virginia development continues — a boundary can run through the middle of a street, and a neighbor's child may attend a different school. Verify your assigned school at fcps.edu using your exact address before signing a lease or finalizing a purchase. A recent boundary adjustment since last year may mean a different school than an online tool or neighbor suggests.

highlights.label: Thomas Jefferson High School for Science and Technology
highlights.detail: TJHSST (commonly called 'TJ') is FCPS's flagship selective high school and is consistently ranked among the top public high schools in the United States. TJ admits students from Fairfax, Arlington, Loudoun, and Prince William counties through a competitive application process. Families with 8th graders interested in TJ should begin researching admission requirements in the fall — applications typically open in October, with testing and evaluations running through winter. TJ acceptance does not affect a student's regular FCPS school assignment until they formally enroll.

highlights.label: FCPS Advanced Academic Programs and Annual Rhythm
highlights.detail: FCPS Advanced Academic Programs (AAP) offer differentiated instruction at all grade levels. Level IV AAP — the most intensive — requires a referral and screening process that typically begins in fall of the prior school year; families who believe their child qualifies should initiate a referral in September or October. The full FCPS annual planning cycle connects these milestones: August 24 school start; September — Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur school holidays, AAP referral window opens; October–November — TJ application period; January — specialty program applications; Spring Break (late March or April — check the current year's FCPS calendar; often coincides with Cherry Blossom peak) — D.C. hotels fill fast; June — last day, one of Virginia's latest. Mapping these in August makes the FCPS year significantly more manageable.

compareIntro: FCPS and its Northern Virginia neighbors — Loudoun and Prince William — share geography but not identical calendars. Spring Break most often aligns across all three districts, but Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur are FCPS holidays that Loudoun and Prince William do not observe, creating no-school days unique to FCPS in September. FCPS also ends its school year in mid-June, later than most Virginia peers. Families coordinating across NoVA district lines for childcare or family schedules should verify each district's full calendar, not just spring break.

relatedDistricts.name: Loudoun County Public Schools
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Loudoun County is FCPS's immediate western neighbor along the Silver Line corridor and the most commonly compared NoVA district. The two districts frequently align on Spring Break week, but Loudoun does not observe Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur as school holidays — creating two FCPS-only no-school days in September with no Loudoun equivalent. Loudoun also tends to end its school year slightly earlier than FCPS. Families with children in both districts, or choosing between them, should pull both official calendars each year rather than assuming the NoVA school schedule is uniform.

relatedDistricts.name: Prince William County Public Schools
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Prince William County sits further from D.C. than Fairfax, with a more economically and demographically mixed community that includes a large military population around Marine Corps Base Quantico. Prince William does not observe Jewish High Holidays as school closures. Spring Break sometimes aligns with FCPS but not always — check both districts each year. Prince William's school year often ends slightly earlier than FCPS's mid-June close. Families relocating between Quantico and the Fairfax corridor are among the most frequent cross-district comparers for this pair.

relatedDistricts.name: Virginia Beach City Public Schools
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Virginia Beach City Public Schools is in Hampton Roads — 200 miles from Fairfax — and operates on a meaningfully different calendar. VBCPS does not observe Jewish High Holidays, ends the school year earlier in June, and faces Atlantic hurricane risks that Fairfax never encounters. Military families transferring between NAS Oceana (Virginia Beach) and NRO, CIA, or Pentagon assignments in Fairfax are the most common reason families compare these two districts. The two school systems' calendars rarely align on spring break.
